1999-12-01.title: Yeltsin's Pneumonia Barely Raises an Eyebrow in Washington
1999-12-01.abstract: Clinton Administration seems unconcerned about Russian Pres Boris N Yeltsin's latest illness, reflecting its view that Yeltsin's health is no longer key to its Russia ties; apparently believes Russia's fledgling democracy is becoming stable enough to outlast him (M)

1999-12-01.title: Mahathir's Party a Big Loser in Malaysian Vote
1999-12-01.abstract: KUALA LUMPU—  The Malaysian general election has produced a convincing victory for the ruling coalition but a striking loss for the party of Prime Minister Mahathir bin Mohamad.

1999-12-01.title: Fearing Instability, Malaysians Keep Leader
1999-12-01.abstract: Malaysian Prime Min Mahathir Mohamad wins new five-year term in election that gives him huge parliamentary majority; voters, apparently fearing instability, reject type of change that has toppled leaders across Asia since economic crisis (M)

1999-12-01.title: A Chaotic Intersection of Tear Gas and Trade Talks
1999-12-01.abstract: Demonstrations in Seattle reduce 'Millennium Round' of trade talks to series of frantic phone calls between hotels as Charlene Barshefsky, United States trade representative, State Sec Madeleine K Albright, United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan and others are unable to leave hotels for meeting sites; Pres Clinton's aides are clearly angered that Seattle authorities failed to prepare secure corridor for negotiators, but do not publicly criticize police; photo (M)

1999-12-01.title: Russians Briefly Detain U.S. Diplomat, Calling Her a Spy
1999-12-01.abstract: Russian Federal Security Service, boasting over prime-time Russian television about its success in unmasking American spies, says it caught Cheri Leberknight, low-level United States diplomat, as she was preparing to meet with Russian informant and confiscated array of espionage equipment; Russia plans protest but does not expect incident to harm US ties; US confirms American diplomat was briefly detained; photo (M)

1999-12-02.title: Clinton Arrives; Seattle Restricts Further Protests : Riots Cast Cloud Over WTO Talks
1999-12-02.abstract: SEATTLE—  President Bill Clinton arrived here Wednesday, in a shaken and subdued city, and immediately sought to move beyond the violent demonstrations that a day earlier disrupted World Trade Organization meetings, hoping to persuade delegates that a deal could still be rescued to further open world markets.

1999-12-02.title: Seeing the Fear of Free Trade Made Concrete
1999-12-02.abstract: Delegates from around world to World Trade Organization talks are surprised that residents of Seattle, port built on international trade, are not strong open trade supporters; see widespread ignorance about trade that could threaten what they had assumed to be implicit consensus in favor of more open borders (M)

1999-12-02.title: Chinese Official Visits Israeli Aircraft Plant
1999-12-02.abstract: Top Chinese official Li Peng concludes five-day visit to Israel with unannounced stop at Israel Aircraft Industries, state company that is outfitting Chinese aircraft with sophisticated surveillance radar; stop takes place against backdrop of continuing United States-Israeli friction over sale to China of advanced Israeli military technology, technology critics hold was developed with American aid; photo (M)

1999-12-02.title: Italian in Libya on First Visit By a Western Leader in 8 Years
1999-12-02.abstract: Italian Prime Min Massimo D'Alema visits Libya, first visit by Western leader in eight years; will meet with Col Muammar el-Qaddafi (M)

1999-12-02.title: China Says Its Computers Are 'Basically Ready' for Year 2000
1999-12-02.abstract: China, after months of belated preparations and testing, says it expects only minimal disruptions from Year 2000 computer breakdowns; notes some poorer provinces in interior and some sectors lag behind (M)

1999-12-03.title: Dark Parallels With Anarchist Outbreaks in Oregon
1999-12-03.abstract: Seattle authorities blame group of young anarchists for causing much of vandalism during demonstrations against World Trade Organization; say some of anarchists come from Eugene, Ore, where they have regular clashes with police; say they cannot state that there is direct connection between Eugene group and chaos in streets; photo (M)

1999-12-03.title: U.S. EFFORT TO ADD LABOR STANDARDS TO AGENDA FAILS
1999-12-03.abstract: Clinton administration fails to rally broad support among nations to add labor standards to World Trade Organization agenda, threatening its most important goal at Seattle meeting that has been plagued by sometimes violent protests; struggles to work out compromise that would have trade organization set up study group on labor standards; Pres Clinton stuns delegates when he says trade group should use sanctions to enforce labor rights around world (M)

1999-12-03.title: NORTHERN IRELAND PICKS UP THE REINS OF ITS GOVERNMENT
1999-12-03.abstract: Northern Ireland gains right to be master of its political fate, no longer ruled directly from London or subject to territorial claims of Dublin; enters era of hoped-for stability during round of meetings and treay signings in Belfast, Dublin and London; under April 1998 peace agreement that goes into effect, home-rule powers move from London to Belfast, Ireland strikes from its Constitution wish to include North, and Protestant and Catholic rivals exercise authority in government jointly for first time; photo (M)

1999-12-03.title: In Seattle, WTO Races the Clock
1999-12-03.abstract: SEATTLE—  With authorities continuing a clampdown on protests Thursday, negotiators worked against the clock to achieve the business for which they had come here: reaching agreement on a new round of global trade negotiations before a Friday deadline.

1999-12-03.title: Germany Widens Investigation of Kohl's Slush Fund
1999-12-03.abstract: PARIS—  A political slush-fund scandal in Germany threatened to spread and engulf the country's main conservative opposition parties Thursday, as the nation's Parliament voted unanimously to open a full-scale inquiry into the admission of improper practices in party funding by former Chancellor Helmut Kohl.

1999-12-04.title: Trade Ministers Sidestep A Sticky Issue: Secrecy
1999-12-04.abstract: Trade ministers fail to take significant action to open veil of secrecy surrounding World Trade Organization proceedings; street protesters in Seattle and Clinton administration repeatedly pressed 135-nation group to end its secretive ways; WTO officials defend their privacy, saying it is government-to-government group that deals in highly controversial and arcane matters; officials fear that if they deliberate in open, many issues would be heavily lobbied by special interests and become far harder to settle (M)

1999-12-04.title: E. J. Safra, 67, Banker and Philanthropist
1999-12-04.abstract: Obituary of Edmond J Safra, banker and philanthropist who died in apparent arson at his Monaco penthouse; he was 67; Safra built vast banking group with Wall Street hustle and old world conservatism; his death comes few weeks after he agreed to sell his banking businesses, including Republic National Bank of New York, to HSBC Holdings for $9.9 billion; he was to get payout of $2.3 billion; Safra, who suffered from Parkinson's disease, planned to retire; he was enduring figure in banking world; photo (M)

1999-12-04.title: Chechen Refugee Convoy Machine-Gunned, Leaving 14 Dead
1999-12-04.abstract: Car and bus convoy of Chechen refugees heading for western border is strafed with machine-gun fire, apparently by Russian helicopters; doctor treating survivors says as many as 14 people died in flaming wreckage; Russian military spokesman says military is investigating matter; Russian forces continue to batter roads leading from Chechen capital, Grozny, including road running through Goyty, where convoy was reported hit; assault on convoy underscores new chorus of warnings, from Western governments and leaders of former Soviet satellites, that Russia's effort to subdue separatist Islamic rebels is indiscriminately wiping out civilians (M)

1999-12-04.title: Mandheera Journal; Somali Militias Now Undergoing 'Rehumanization'
1999-12-04.abstract: Former members of Somali militias, once a symbol of anarchy, are training to become useful members of society as police officers, soldiers and prison guards in territory that calls itself Republic of Somaliland and has declared itself separate from rest of Somalia; Somaliland is, in fact, peaceful, but militia program is viewed as essentially a bribe for peace, because Somaliland government spends 73 percent of its revenue on maintaining this huge force of former militia members; officials in Somaliland are trying to persuade outside world to help it attain independence; other countries are reluctant to recognize Somaliland as independent country or to send money to help it develop independent economy; photo; map (M)

1999-12-04.title: Mexico Suspends Plan For Hefty Deposit on Cars
1999-12-04.abstract: Mexican government scraps plan for deposits of up to $800 from travelers crossing border from United States with vehicles not registered in Mexico; program, which lasted just two days, had caused outcry from lawmakers and community leaders representing Mexicans in US planning to return to homeland for holidays (S)

1999-12-05.title: MONITORS' REPORTS PROVIDE CHRONICLE OF KOSOVO TERROR
1999-12-05.abstract: Organization for Security and Cooperation Europe compiles two detailed chronicles of human rights abuses in Kosovo, which present depressing picture of an ugly war, full of individual and collective cruelty by Serbs, followed by an ugly peace displaying many of the same depredations, if on a smaller scale, by the province's Albanians; provide overwhelming evidence of a Serbian campaign, organized by a powerful, authoritarian state and its security forces, to drive nearly one million Albanians from the province; photos (M)

1999-12-05.title: New Reports Back Claims of Attack on Chechen Refugee Convoy
1999-12-05.abstract: New accounts from survivors bolster charges that Russian soldiers killed some 40 civilians on Dec 3 in an attack on convoy of refugees from Grozny, Chechnya; Russian troops complete their encirclement of city (M)

1999-12-05.title: The Shipwreck in Seattle
1999-12-05.abstract: David E Sanger analysis of Clinton administration's miscalculations in pursuing global trade talks in Seattle; says administration chose a spectacularly bad moment to pick this particular fight (M)

1999-12-05.title: The Shape Of Justice Is Undefined In Cambodia
1999-12-05.abstract: Cambodia is moving toward some sort of trials for some of former Khmer Rouge even though Prime Min Hun Sen had hoped to 'dig a hole and bury the past'; contentious questions still remain over whether any such trials will meet international standards of fairness and legal rigor; critics fear outcomes will be crafted to suit Hun Sen's political needs rather than to examine the deaths of more than a million people by Khmer Rouge (M)

1999-12-05.title: Safra, Fearing Intruders, Could Have Saved Himself
1999-12-05.abstract: French police says billionaire banker Edmond J Safra died of smoke inhalation in his Monte Carlo penthouse because--even after the police and firefighter reached penthouse--he still believed that he was in danger from masked intruders and refused to leave the bathroom where he had barricaded himself with one of his nurses; are questioning his other nurse, who was in the infirmary when the bizarre incident that led to Safra's death began (M)

1999-12-06.title: Bangkok Opens Skytrain, but Will It Ease Car Traffic?
1999-12-06.abstract: Long-delayed Skytrain, 16-mile, $1.7 billion elevated rail system begins service in Bangkok, Thailand; privately-financed project is city's most ambitious attempt so far to confront near-gridlock that is sometimes described as its defining landmark; photo (M)

1999-12-06.title: Iraq Opens a War of Words Against Its French Friends
1999-12-06.abstract: Government of Iraq threatens France with retaliation against its diplomats and oil companies if it supports proposal in UN Security Council to vote on new arms inspection system; Western diplomats say Iraq is in effect asking France and Russian, which have considerable financial interests there, to choose between potential business deals and good relations with US and number of European countries also pressing for new inspections (M)

1999-12-06.title: Mozambique Enlists Healers in AIDS Prevention
1999-12-06.abstract: Curandeiros, traditional healers banned by government of Mozambique for nearly two decades of civil war, are being embraced as vital bearers of safe-sex message in fight against AIDS; government will link teams of trained healers directly to hospitals, which have been overwhelmed by influx of patients infected with HIV; healers will begin treating patients at home, freeing up hospital beds for other sick people; will use herbs and plants that health officials say effectively treat opportunistic diseases that afflicts those whose immune systems are impaired by AIDS; photos; map (M)

1999-12-06.title: J.F. Wilson, 80, Whose Work Saved Millions From Blindness
1999-12-06.abstract: Sir John Foster Wilson, who was blinded by a Bunsen burner exploding in his face as a 12-year-old schoolboy and went on to lead campaigns to save millions from blindness, died in his home in Brighton, England on Nov. 24. He was 80.    Sir John's principal vehicle was Sight Savers International, which he originally called the Royal Commonwealth Society for the Blind. Since the early 1980's, he had expanded his personal crusade to help people with all types of disabilities. His work extended from Africa to India to the Caribbean.

1999-12-06.title: Holbrooke to Draw Outline Of New U.S. Plans in Africa
1999-12-06.abstract: Interview with Richard C Holbrooke, US Ambassador to UN, who says Clinton administration is weighing series of new initiatives for Africa; will lay out new policies in December 6 speech in Pretoria, South Africa (M)

1999-12-07.title: World Briefing
1999-12-07.abstract: AFRICA    RWANDA: GUILTY OF GENOCIDE -- A United Nations war crimes tribunal convicted a leader of a Hutu militia that helped lead the attempt to destroy the ethnic Tutsi in Rwanda in 1994. Georges Rutaganda, 43, an urbane businessman and son of a diplomat, was found guilty of genocide, crimes against humanity and murder in the 100 days of massacres in which at least 500,000 Tutsi died. Mr. Rutaganda, who was sentenced to life in prison, is the sixth person found guilty since the tribunal began hearings in Arusha, Tanzania.   Ian Fisher (NYT)

1999-12-07.title: Nurse Is Said to Admit Arson That Killed Banker in Monaco
1999-12-07.abstract: Daniel Serdet, Monaco's chief prosecutor, says Ted Maher, American nurse who worked for Edmond J Safra, has admitted setting fire that killed billionaire banker in his Monte Carlo penthouse; says Maher told police that he had not intended to kill anyone and had fabricated his story that apartment was under attack by two hooded men with knives; says Maher 'wanted to be a hero'; says Safra and his nurse, Vivienne Torrent, could have escaped, but banker, fearing that assailants were after him, refused to leave bathroom where he took refuge even when police and firemen arrived on scene; says Maher will be charged with arson without intent to kill; photo (M)

1999-12-07.title: Russians Issue An Ultimatum To Rebel City
1999-12-07.abstract: Russian military drops thousands of leaflets over Grozny, warning residents of besieged city to get out by Dec 11 or risk death; ultimatum underscores resolve of Russian generals to take Chechen capital, either by force or by shelling it into submission; Nikolai Koshman, deputy prime minister who has been put in charge of Chechnya, says 45,000 to 50,000 civilians remain in city; Russian military officials say 5,000 to 7,000 rebel fighters have also taken refuge in Grozny, where 250,000 people lived before war; map; photos (M)

1999-12-07.title: Canada Split As Gun Laws Are Tightened
1999-12-07.abstract: Canadians, decade after Marc Lepine murdered 14 women at University of Montreal, are grappling with massacre's legislative legacy--strict federal laws that mandate registration of all guns and licensing of gun owners; many Canadians in west and north, which have nation's highest rates of gun ownership, say they will not register their guns (M)

1999-12-07.title: Tokyo Journal; Exam Wars, Prepping and Other Nursery Crimes
1999-12-07.abstract: Recent murder in Tokyo of Haruna Wakayama, 2-year-old girl who was killed by jealous mother of classmate at preparatory nursery school, has shocked many people; killing has inspired scores of editorials about 'examination wars' that mark progression from kindergarten to 12th grade of every Japanese child; photo (M)

1999-12-08.title: Where Most See Ramparts, North Korea Imagines a Wall
1999-12-08.abstract: North Korean diplomat Chu Chang Hun uses rare news conference in Beijing to rail against nonexistent 150-mile-long concrete 'wall of division' that South Koreans supposedly erected 20 years ago across buffer zone between North and South (S)

1999-12-08.title: After Delay, Serbs Release Western Fuel For 2 Towns
1999-12-08.abstract: Serbian customs officials release 14 tankers of heating fuel from European Union for towns of Nis and Pirot that had been blocked at border for nearly two weeks; map (M)

1999-12-08.title: Barak Pledges to Halt Expansion Of Settlements in the West Bank
1999-12-08.abstract: Israeli Prime Ehud Barak moves to defuse growing tensions with Palestinians on eve of visit by US Sec Madeleine K Albright by pledging to halt further expansion of Jewish settlements in West Bank; stops short of baldly declaring freeze on construction in unsuccessful attempt to avoid provoking right-wing members of his coalition government; calls moratorium of several months on new settlers' neighborhoods to avert confrontation in delicate period, as parties seek to reach framework on final peace agreement by February 2000; spokesman acknowledges that work already in progress, approved by previous administration, will continue; photo (M)

1999-12-08.title: Russia Uses a Sledgehammer In Chechnya War This Time
1999-12-08.abstract: Russian forces are using sledgehammer approach in breakaway province of Chechnya, applying overwhelming and massive firepower in efforts to rout Islamic rebels; while Western critics complain that artillery and air strikes have unnecessarily harmed civilians, they have been important part of Russian military's strategy, unlike 1994-96 war, to limit its casualties and maintain support at home; capture of village of Primykaniye after furious 10-hour firefight described; photos; map (M)

1999-12-08.title: World Briefing
1999-12-08.abstract: EUROPE    THE NETHERLANDS: APPEAL IN LOCKERBIE TRIAL -- Lawyers for two Libyans accused of planting the bomb that downed Pan Am Flight 103 over Scotland asked the court to drop conspiracy charges. The hearing was the first appearance of the suspects, Abdel Basset Ali al-Megrahi and Al-Amin Khalifa Fhimah, since they were handed over in April. They are to be tried under Scottish law in the Netherlands, a neutral venue, on charges of murder, conspiracy to murder and other violations in the 270 deaths. Their lawyers argued that the alleged bomb preparations did not occur in Scotland.   (AP)

1999-12-09.title: Clinton's Words to Press: On the Mideast, Chechnya and Other Matters
1999-12-09.abstract: Excerpts from Pres Clinton's news conference (L)

1999-12-09.title: Trapped by War and Politics In a Cheerless Chechen Town
1999-12-09.abstract: Residents of Znamenskoye in northwest Chechnya accept Russian troop presence with weary resignation, noting troops have brought welcome measure of stability; say they felt caught in middle between Russia and Chechen rebels who previously controlled town and abused by both sides; map; photos (M)

1999-12-09.title: Pearl Harbor Truly a Sneak Attack, Papers Show
1999-12-09.abstract: Newly discovered Japanese diplomatic papers published in Japan seem to refute widely held belief that Japan attacked Pearl Harbor in Dec 1941 without official warning of break in ties only because of fateful accidents and bumbling that delayed delivery of document to Washington hinting at war; indicate Japan intentionally sought to conceal its hostile intentions from US after vigorous internal debate over how and whether to notify US of plan to start war; photo (M)

1999-12-09.title: Russia and Belarus Agree To Join in a Confederation
1999-12-09.abstract: Russian Pres Boris Yeltsin and Belarus Pres Aleksandr Lukashenko, after years of talks, agree in principle to form economic and political confederation, rite, Moscow; announce sheaf of related initiatives, including plan to create joint army district; Russian leaders seem unlikely to carry out many of alliance's grand designs soon, if ever; Lukashenko holds new district is designed to defend against invasion from West; Yeltsin says confederation will keep nations separate and is not targeted at anyone; rite symbolizes Russia's implict turn inward, further away from Western influence; map; photo (M)

1999-12-09.title: Ousted Pakistan Leader Charged With Treason and Other Crimes
1999-12-09.abstract: Pakistani prosecutors charge ousted prime minister, Nawaz Sharif, and six others with hijacking, kidnapping, attempted murder and plotting to wage war against state; Sharif complains he is being kept out of touch, in solitary confinement in cramped cell, unable to meet with lawyers, wife or brother Shahbaz, who is co-defendant; case stems from Sharif's alledged effort to bar safe landing of commercial jet carrying Gen Pervez Musharraf to Karachi after Sharif dismissed him as army chief of staff; jet was allowed to land only after army seized control of airport (M)

1999-12-10.title: Syrians Say Sky Is Clear On Horizon For New Talks
1999-12-10.abstract: Syrian government and its Arab allies speak with customary caution even as they welcomed planned resumption of peace talks between Syria and Israel; but across Arab world, there are also rare hints of haste to learn whether breakthrough might bear real fruit (M)

1999-12-10.title: U.S. Told to Spend More to Neutralize Soviet Germ Scientists
1999-12-10.abstract: Henry L Stimson Center, Washington research group, asserts that Clinton Adm has made 'impressive strides' in preventing former Soviet scientists from working for rogue states and terrorists seeking unconventional biological and chemical weapons, but it should spend much more to achieve that aim (M)

1999-12-10.title: Angry Yeltsin Reacts To Clinton Criticism
1999-12-10.abstract: Boris N. Yeltsin, bristling at President Clinton's criticism of Russia's bombardment of civilian areas in secessionist Chechnya, complained that the United States was not treating Russia with the respect due a nuclear power. In Beijing to meet with Chinese leaders, Mr. Yeltsin said Mr. Clinton had ''for a minute forgotten that Russia has a full arsenal of nuclear weapons.''     Article, Page A14.

1999-12-10.title: Europe Chiefs Meet, Dogged By Chechnya And Disputes
1999-12-10.abstract: European Union leaders gather in Helsinki for summit conference aimed at reforming and reshaping their organization, but on eve of two-day meeting, they are distracted by alarm over Russian assault on Chechnya and a sudden outbreak of quarrels among member nations (M)

1999-12-10.title: State Dept. Now Estimates Serbian Drive Killed 10,000
1999-12-10.abstract: US State Department estimates that 10,000 Kosovar Albanians were killed this year in Serbian campaign to force out Albanian population, a figure nearly five times larger than number of bodies unearthed from mass graves; in report on toll from so-called ethnic cleansing of Kosovo, department also says that 1.5 million Albanians had been expelled from their houses and that more than 2,000 Albanians were in custody in Serbian detention camps; some Balkan experts and others sympathetic to Serbs have described estimates as exaggerated (M)

1999-12-11.title: Short Guide to Finding Bargains On-Line
1999-12-11.abstract: PARIS—  The Internet offers travelers an enormous resource for finding bargains, for making reservations and for obtaining up-to-the-minute information that printed guidebooks cannot match. As a result, the travel industry is fast becoming a major player on the World Wide Web.

1999-12-11.title: Historians' Report Blames Swiss for Barring Jews During War
1999-12-11.abstract: Panel of independent historians concludes that wartime Swiss officials refused entry to thousands of Jewish refugees even after it was known that they faced almost certain death in Nazi Germany; blames anti-Semitism and an entrenched fear of foreigners in Switzrland; says there is no evidence that accepting many more asylum seekers would have put neutral Switzerland in danger of invasion by Axis or caused insurmountable economic difficulties (M)

1999-12-11.title: Turkey Accepts European Union's Offer but Without Jubilation
1999-12-11.abstract: Turkey welcomes European Union's acceptance of its candidacy for membership, but some Turks are puzzled with conditions that seem to be attached to the invitation, which might require Turkey to chanage its approach to contentious issues like Cyprus and the Aegean islands it contest with Greece (M)

1999-12-11.title: Panama Feels Slighted When Albright Cancels Date at Canal
1999-12-11.abstract: Sec of State Madeleine Albright, who was set to head US delegation at symbolic handover of Panama Canal to Panama after Pres Clinton backed out, will attend Mideast peace talks in Washington instead; US delegation at Dec 14 ceremony will now be led by former Pres Jimmy Carter; Panamanian officials feel slighted; photo (M)

1999-12-11.title: China Signs Pact to Give Serbia $300 Million for Reconstruction
1999-12-11.abstract: China will advance Yugoslavia $300 million in cash and credits to aid reconstruction after NATO bombing (S)

1999-12-12.title: Congo Leader Promises Cooperation With Mediator in Civil War
1999-12-12.abstract: Pres Laurent Kabila of Congo tells Richad C Holbrooke, US representative at UN, that he would be willing to work with an African mediator to find a political settlement to the civil war in his country; Holbrooke says all sides and their supporters now appear ready to discuss a political solution under auspices of Organization of African Unity, which has so far failed to appoint an envoy called for in July peace agreement (M)

1999-12-12.title: Mexican Students in Clash at U.S. Embassy
1999-12-12.abstract: A melee erupted today between students and the police during a demonstration against the World Trade Organization in front of the United States Embassy. The police said 70 protesters were arrested, 6 policemen were injured by knives or rocks and 18 embassy windows were damaged.    The police reported that the protest was led by students who are participating in a strike, now nearly eight months old, at the National Autonomous University of Mexico. But the demonstration was not related to the strike. The protesters called for the release of prisoners arrested during the demonstrations in Seattle against the recent trade organization meeting.

1999-12-12.title: For China's Rural Migrants, an Education Wall
1999-12-12.abstract: Tens of millions of rural Chinese who seek better lives in cities are shortchanged on education; cities like Beijing permit children of migrants, most of whom are actually longtime resident working low-end jobs, attend public schools only if they pay stiff fees that few can afford; unlicensed schools, some run by people with no more than a junior high school education, have grown up to serve these families, but they receive no government aid; China's system of residential permits and the privileges of official urban residents, who make up less than one-third of national population, seem certain to rankle more and more in the years ahead, especially as global competition kills off marginal producers and forces more Chinese from farms and to the cities; photo (M)

1999-12-12.title: Unicef Says War Affects Poor Children In the Millions
1999-12-12.abstract: Unicef says the lives of millions of the world's poorest people, mostly children and their monthers, have been set back by rash of brutish, primitive wars, poverty and the spread of the virus that causes AIDS (M)

1999-12-12.title: Under Attack, Leading Rival Of Milosevic Joins the Fray
1999-12-12.abstract: Serbian Renewal Party led by Vuk Draskovic moves into open opposition to Pres Slobodan Milosevic, apparently stung by state security officials who responded to the party's accusation that they orchestrated a campaign of state terrorism with harsh court rulings and police summons to party leaders (M)

1999-12-13.title: Fallout From 'Brute Force' / 2 Inquiries Into Rights Abuses : East Timorese Hope for Justice, but Jakarta Holds Back
1999-12-13.abstract: DILI, East Timor—  On a bright sunny day recently, Jose Ramos-Horta walked through the long grass of the Santa Cruz cemetery past tightly packed headstones to find two particular graves. One marked the resting place of his elder brother, Antonio, who died in 1994; the other belonged to Sebastiao Gomes, a student activist for independence from Indonesia whose death led to a massacre by Indonesian troops that caused outrage around the world.

1999-12-13.title: Though Grieving for Tudjman, Many Croats Want Change
1999-12-13.abstract: Many Croats who mourn death of Pres Franjo Tudjman will nevertheless vote for opposition in coming parliamentary election on Jan 3; are unhappy with wretched economy, high taxes, late pension and salary payments and widespread corruption; six-party opposition expects to win election; photo (M)

1999-12-13.title: Russian Politicians Feeding On the Misery in Chechnya
1999-12-13.abstract: Russian politicians flock to Chechnya to demonstrate their concern for the Russian troops fighting Islamic rebels and to bask in Russian television coverage of a war that has strong public support; Sergei Shoigu, leader of the Kremlin-backed Unity party, makes a press event out of a mission ostensibly aimed at inaugurating a new corridor to allow civilians to escape Grozny, Chechen capital; Prime Min Vladimir V Putin boosts his standing in polls not only by ordering campaign against rebels but also by standing up to Western critics of it; photos; map (M)

1999-12-13.title: The Big Ditch: Panama's Big Opportunity
1999-12-13.abstract: Panama has ambitious plans to convert properties along Panama Canal into commercial hubs and tropical tourist oasis; hopes to create more jobs than US military presence there ever did; acknowledges that that full control of canal and its properties brings with it enormous challenges and responsibilities for a country where corruption and partisan feuds have marked much of its political history; must also develop its own national security plans and foreign policy to protect its borders and the canal and assure canal's users that it will remain safe and above board; photos; map (M)

1999-12-13.title: Syria Says Peace Deal With Israel Could Be Reached Soon
1999-12-13.abstract: Foreign Min Farouk al-Shara of Syria expresses confidence that a peace deal with Israel can be concluded quickly, perhaps in a matter of months; Prime Min Ehud Barak tells Israeli cabinet that negotiations with Syria can be completed in a few weeks; Pres Emile Lahoud of Lebanon, however, says new talks are bound to be difficult (M)

1999-12-14.title: Security Council Backs Off Again on Vote on Inspections for Iraq
1999-12-14.abstract: UN Security Council backs away again from vote on new arms-inspection system for Iraq (S)

1999-12-14.title: U.S. Reports Mideast Arrests Of New Year's Terror Suspects
1999-12-14.abstract: Clinton administration announces arrest of about 12 members of suspected terrorist operation believed to be planning New Year's attacks on Americans around world; arrests are in Middle East and suspects are linked to Osama bin Laden; State Department still advises Americans to stay away from large celebrations overseas, through New Year and Ramadan (S)

1999-12-14.title: ISRAEL LAWMAKERS CLOSELY  APPROVE TALKS WITH SYRIA
1999-12-14.abstract: Israeli parliament approves Prime Min Ehud Barak's peace negotiations with Syria, after six hours of impassioned debate; photos; Barak, in emotional plea, acknowledges nation will have to pay 'heavy territorial price,' but warns that failure 'could cost us in blood'; vote is 47-31 with 24 abstentions, far from strong majority Barak had sought; three allied parties refuse support; Pres Clinton is to host opening of talks in Washington; Barak argues that Israel has chance to make lasting peace with all immediate neighbors, achieving Zionist dream; says precise boundaries will be determined by 'depth of peace' and quality of security, echoing formula of late Prime Min Yitzhak Rabin; thousands of residents of northern border zone demonstrate angrily outside; opposition leader Ariel Sharon predicts deal featuring Golan Heights withdrawal will be defeated in referendum (M)

1999-12-14.title: Snubbed by the West, Tudjman Receives a Posthumous Rebuke
1999-12-14.abstract: Croatian Pres Franjo Tudjman is buried in somber ceremony, mourned by tens of thousands of Croats to whom he helped deliver independence, and by less-than-stellar panoply of foreign dignitaries (S)

1999-12-14.title: As Panamanians See It, Clinton Is a Party Pooper
1999-12-14.abstract: Panamanian Foreign Min Jose Miguel Aleman offers some decidedly undiplomatic observations about absence of Pres Clinton and Sec Madeleine Albright from ceremony marking handover of Panama Canal; also denounces 'lies' in US Congress about alleged risk that China will control canal (S)

1999-12-15.title: Mirror on the Past: Canal's Fate Reflects Shift by U.S.
1999-12-15.abstract: News analysis of American transfer of full sovereignty over Panama Canal to government of Panama after nearly century of US control; out of 20th century sense of sovereignty and respect for other nation's national pride, great power is voluntarily giving up piece of valuable national property in small nation with no capacity to seize it (M)

1999-12-15.title: Where War Roiled Danube, Nature Is Peacemaker
1999-12-15.abstract: Hungary, Yugoslavia and Croatia will establish nature preserve at confluence of Danube and Drava rivers where borders of three nations meet; vast flood plain, which is Central Europe's largest wetland and home to large variety of wildlife, was front line in war of early 1990's when Croatia broke away from Yugoslavia; cross-border nature reserve will encompass 250,000 acres; photos; map (M)

1999-12-15.title: The Mideast's Moment
1999-12-15.abstract: Israel and its neighbors have arrived at a critical turning point. When the Syrian foreign minister greets Israel's prime minister at the White House today, Syria will be taking a giant step toward recognizing the state of Israel.

1999-12-15.title: GERMANS TO SET UP $5.1 BILLION FUND FOR NAZIS' SLAVES
1999-12-15.abstract: German industry and government officials, after months of bitter negotiations, will establish $5.1 billion fund to compensate victims of Nazi slave labor during World War II; settlement could untimately affect more than one million surviving victims of Nazi's systematic use of forced labor; US estimates there are 240,000 surviving slave laborers, about half Jewish, who were held in concentration camps and forced to work under nightmarish conditions; fund would also compensate much larger number, most of whom were not Jewish and were brought from Soviet Union, Poland, and other Eastern European countries; officials estimate group at 700,000 to 1.5 million people (M)

1999-12-15.title: Israel Says It Killed Two During Raid At Bomb Shop
1999-12-15.abstract: Israeli Army officials say two members of militant Palestinian group Hamas were killed by Israeli commandos near Hebron as they prepared bomb attack (S)

1999-12-16.title: U.N. Agrees to Share Power With Kosovars
1999-12-16.abstract: UN agrees to power-sharing pact with three Kosovo Albanian leaders, bringing them into official administration to help govern unruly Serbian province; deal gives Kosovars executive role in governing province and official status and council seats to Hashim Thaci, Ibrahim Rugova and Rexhep Qosja; UN officials say accord will dismantle unofficial local structures that Albanians have been running and break up political rivalries that have frequently stymied UN administration (M)y

1999-12-16.title: Damascus Encourages Optimism With Limits
1999-12-16.abstract: Syrians, driven from Golan Heights by Israel in 1967, are transfixed on Washington, where peace talk have resumed after four-year hiatus;Syrian Government has been encouraging optimism, while also trying to keep it in check; photo (M)

1999-12-16.title: Top Court in Hong Kong Upholds Ban on Defacing Chinese Flag
1999-12-16.abstract: Hong Kong's top court steers clear of another legal confrontation with Chinese government, ruling that defacing Chinese flag is crime; decision, victory for local government, reverses ruling by lower court that said defacing flag was form of freedom of expression guaranteed in constitution of former British colony (S)

1999-12-16.title: Belgium Pedophilia Scandal /Did Authorities Cover Up Its Scope?: Book Revives Fear of Grand Conspiracy
1999-12-16.abstract: BRUSSELS—  In Belgium, the X-Files refer not to the U.S. television series but to a series of horrific witness accounts of an alleged pedophile network.

1999-12-16.title: Venezuelans Give Chavez All the Powers He Wanted
1999-12-16.abstract: Venezuelans vote overwhelmingly in favor of new Constitution that gives Pres Hugo Chavez sweeping powers he says are needed to transform country's political and social structure in 'peaceful revolution;' new Constitution, drawn up by assembly in which Chavez supporters hold more than 90 percent of seats, increases his term of office from five years to six and allows him to succeed himself; eliminates one chamber of Congress, reduces civilian control of military and significantly expands government management of economy--which is based on largest reserves of oil outside Middle East (M)

1999-12-17.title: Remarks From the Two U.S. Players
1999-12-17.abstract: Excerpts from Pres Clinton's statement on Mideast and State Sec Madeleine K Albright's news conf (M)

1999-12-17.title: Paying for Peace Is Clinton's Challenge
1999-12-17.abstract: Any agreement reached in Israel-Syria talks is certain to entail huge costs--to monitor security arrangements, to relocate Israeli settlers and to bolster Syrian economy; United States will bear lion's share of burden, if past is any guide (M)

1999-12-17.title: Fear of Losing Out in Race for Damascus
1999-12-17.abstract: Palestinians are concerned that they may be left in dust as Prime Minister Ehud Barak goes off in pursuit of better relations with Syria, after nearly four years of being sole Arab partner in peace talks with Israel (S)

1999-12-17.title: Chechens Say They Were Shot At in Safe Corridor
1999-12-17.abstract: Desperate civilians fleeing blistering Russian bombardment of Grozny say they had to run gantlet of gunfire in 'humanitarian corridor' set up by Russian Army; it is not clear who was doing shooting; refugees interviewed at checkpoint six miles from Grozny say bus full of refugees was riddled with machine-gun fire, leaving four people wounded, and at least one car bursts into flames (M)

1999-12-17.title: Europe Court Faults Trial Of Boy Killers Of Toddler
1999-12-17.abstract: European Court of Justice rules that two 10-year-old boys who kidnapped and killed 2-year-old James Bulger in suburban Liverpool shopping center in 1993 had not received fair trial in Britain; judges hold that boys, Jon Venables and Robert Thompson, had been too young to take part effectively in procedure meant for adults and that home secretary at time, Michael Howard, had erred in increasing their eight-year sentence to 15 years; boys, now in their mid-teens, have served six years, and while decision does not affect their convictions, it could allow lawyers to seek their release in British courts; case reviewed (M)

1999-12-18.title: Rich Nations Fault Russia On Chechnya; Call for Truce
1999-12-18.abstract: World's biggest industrial countries, meeting with Russia in Group of Eight, issues another condemnation of Moscow's actions in Chechnya and calls for immediate cease-fire; Secretary of State Madeleine K Albright warns that Russia is becoming increasingly isolated in international community (M)

1999-12-18.title: Syria Talks Breed Hope: Lebanese Refrain From Revenge
1999-12-18.abstract: Barrage by Israeli-backed troops that injured 20 children in southern Lebanon has not brought retaliatory strike into Israel by Lebanese guerrillas; restraint is apparently under pressure from Syria, which is in peace talks in Washington with Israel; map (M)

1999-12-18.title: Divided U.N. Council Approves New Iraq Arms Inspection Plan
1999-12-18.abstract: Security Council votes to create new arms inspection system for Iraq, with promise to Pres Saddam Hussein that sanctions could be suspended within a year and eventually lifted if he cooperates; plan immediately removes ceiling on amount of oil Iraq may sell abroad to buy food and relaxes controls on import of certain supplies; Iraq's United Nations envoy Saeed Hassan, rejects plan, saying government will not permit any UN inspectors back into Iraq and demanding unconditional end to sanctions; photo (M)

1999-12-18.title: Robert W. Maxwell, 78, U.N. Postal Chief
1999-12-18.abstract: Robert W Maxwell, United Nations Postal Administration chief from 1966 to 1971, dies at age 78 (S)

1999-12-18.title: U.N. Resolution on New Monitors in Iraq
1999-12-18.abstract: Excerpts from Security Council resolution on Iraq

1999-12-19.title: Russia Voting After Rancorous Campaign, but All's Just Prelude to Main Bout Next Year
1999-12-19.abstract: Communist party is expected to win plurality of 450 seats at stake in elections for lower house of Russian Parliament, but focus of elections is really on whether second place will go to the Kremlin-backed party, variously called Unity and Medved (Bear), or party led by former Prime Min Yevgeny M Primakov and Mayor Yuri D Luzhkov of Moscow, which is called Fatherland-All Russia; parliamentary elections are seen primarily as early clue to presidential election in 2000, when Primakov will face the Kremlin-backed candidate, Prime Min Vladimir M Putin; photos (M)

1999-12-19.title: Chretien Takes Risk For Unity Of Canada
1999-12-19.abstract: Prime Min Jean Chretien makes bold gamble with Canada's future by introducing legislation that authorizes House of Commons in Ottawa to rule on clarity of any secessionist question for Quebec and to decide whether results of a referendum constitute a clear majority; this offends Quebecers of many political stripes who believe that it is up to their provincial legislature to set the rules for a referendum that would decide their political future; Quebec separatists would prefer to have a referendum phrased ambiguously (M)

1999-12-19.title: Now Chavez Takes On The Church In Venezuela
1999-12-19.abstract: Pres Hugh Chavez engages in war of words with Venezuela's Roman Catholic hierarchy; immediate cause of tension is new voter-approved Constitution, which does not assert rights of the unborn as clearly and completely as church leaders would like and which describes education as primarily a responsibility of the state; dispute arises as Chavez's wife, Marisabel, seeks annulment of her first marriage so that she and Chavez can have a formal church wedding (M)

1999-12-19.title: The Communist Party: Holding Fast to a Well-Worn Theme
1999-12-19.abstract: THE SCENE -- A muscular worker's arm in a plaid shirt slams a hard hat on a table atop a copy of a Communist newspaper. An arm attired in a white shirt with gold cufflinks and a Rolex watch follows, depositing a mobile phone on the table. Sleeves rolled up, the two begin to arm wrestle. As years flash on the screen -- 1991, 1993, 1995 -- the white-shirted arm slowly presses the worker's arm downward. By 1997, the worker begins to come back, and the white-shirted figure tries to overcome him with both hands. But as 1999 flashes by, the worker wins.    THE SCRIPT -- A Soviet war medal appears on the screen, and to the sound of a war anthem, an announcer intones: ''For victory! The Communist Party of the Russian Federation!''

1999-12-19.title: 1999 Continues Warming Trend Around Globe
1999-12-19.abstract: Global warming trend continue in 1999, which is certain to join 1998 as one of the two warmest years on record; 1999 may be slightly less warm that record year of 1998 because of general cooling influence of La Nina phenomenon in eastern tropical Pacific; La Nina is expected to produce same variable weather pattern as it did last winter, and experts say that if atmospheric conditions in North Atlantic develop in a certain way, mid-Atlantic coast, including New York region, could see some heavy snow; photos; maps (L)

1999-12-20.title: Life, and Death, in Chechnya's Capital
1999-12-20.abstract: Mikhail A Kashpurin, a 76-year-old ethnic Russian, describes the harrowing bombardment of Grozny, the Chechen capital, by Russian forces, his efforts to escape bands of Islamic militants roaming city's streets and his ultimate escape; Russian missile killed his wife; photo; maps (M)

1999-12-20.title: Barak Hopes Syria Will Agree on Core Issues
1999-12-20.abstract: Prime Min Ehud Barak tells his cabinet that Israel will seek an initial core agreement summarizing the main issues with Syria when the two sides start intensive talks near Washington on Jan 3; says details of full peace treaty will be fleshed out later; suggests that reaching core agreement should not take months (M)

1999-12-20.title: Israel Details Pope's Visit in March
1999-12-20.abstract: Israel confirms that in March Pope John Paul II will make first official visit to Israel by a pope; his six-day visit will take in Christian holy sites, Yad Vashem, the Holocaust memorial, and perhaps the Western Wall in Jerusalem; photo (M)

1999-12-20.title: Minna Journal; Butting Their Heads Against Religion and Men
1999-12-20.abstract: Nigerian girls wanting to play soccer face obstacles despite strong showing by Nigerian team in this year's women's World Cup competition; soccer is associated with the barefoot poor; Muslims in northern part of country fear that soccer, with its revealing shorts, will encourage immorality or that playing might injure girls or make them infertile; photo; map (M)

1999-12-20.title: Japan Carries Out Executions in Near-Secrecy
1999-12-20.abstract: Japan executes two murderers with its accustomed near-secrecy; is the only advanced industrialized country besides the US that still executes prisoners, and although US executes far more people each year, critics of death penalty in Japan say Japan is distinguished by unusual cruelty in conditions of detention and the near-secret application of capital punishment (M)

1999-12-21.title: NATO Arrests Serb Ex-General on War Crimes Charges
1999-12-21.abstract: NATO peacekeepers in Bosnia arrest former Bosnian Serb general, Stanislav Galic, on war crimes charges; British troops block vehicle and seize Galic on sealed indictment issued by international tribunal; Gen Wesley K Clark says arrest vindicates standing orders on dealing with suspects, but 12 of 15 arrests so far have been made by British units; Galic is charged with ordering shelling that killed and and maimed thousands of civilians in Sarajevo; Clark, in France, also reveals that NATO was close to mounting ground attack against Serbs during war (M)

1999-12-21.title: German General's Kosovo Peacekeepers Are Fighting Crime
1999-12-21.abstract: Gen Klaus Reinhardt, NATO commander in Kosovo, orders troops into streets to fight crime and violence in absence of effective police force; map; calls on nations to contribute more people to international police force, but says in meantime soldiers will have to combat ethnic killings and retaliation and increased crime among Albanians; moves troops from calm areas to 'fault lines' with more violence (M)

1999-12-21.title: Russia Votes, Like It or Not
1999-12-21.abstract: News analysis of Russian elections which, while certainly democratic, resulted in millions of voters demonstrating their support for brutal war in Chechnya by voting for hawkish Prime Min Vladimir Putin; widespread support shown for war, and Putin's soaring popularity, not only reduces chance for political settlement in Chechnya, but also suggests broad shift in Russian public sentiment toward more nationalist, if pro-capitalist, stance; analyst Andrei Piontkovsky observes that democracy is no guarantee against 'nasty things'; Putin, former intelligence chief untainted by corruption charges, benefits from opinion that Chechnya is haven for terrorists and also from Russian hankering for strongman (M)

1999-12-21.title: Chief American U.N. Delegate Charts Course for His Month at the Helm
1999-12-21.abstract: UN Amb Richard Holbrooke, confirmed after long delay in Senate, hopes to use his year in post to put imprint on Washington's relations with world; Holbrooke holds news conference to discuss agenda for January, when he will takes on rotating presidency of Security Council; photo; he points out that UN still needs much reform (M)

1999-12-21.title: Venezuela Struggles to Calculate Toll From Floods and Mudslides
1999-12-21.abstract: Venezuelan authorities struggle to rescue survivors of coastal mudslides and flooding; estimates of dead vary widely, reflecting chaotic conditions (S)

1999-12-22.title: Investor Is Accused of Fraud Over Azerbaijan Venture
1999-12-22.abstract: Lawsuit filed in London against investment promoter Viktor Kozeny charges fraud related to long-stalled privatization program of Azerbaijan; Kozeny made fortune from questionable stock dealings in his native Czech Republic; photo; latest venture attracted $450 million from foreign investors, including George J Mitchell and Leon G Cooperman; suit is filed by Marlwood Commercial unit of American International Group, seeking $15 million to recoup company's investment (M)

1999-12-22.title: Former Aide To Franco Challenged In Galicia
1999-12-22.abstract: Fierce political battle to succeed Manuel Fraga Iribarne as president of Galicia in northwest Spain looms between conservatives and upstart progressives, who for first time won control of most of Galicia's big cities in June; Iribarne, who turned 77 in November and has ruled Galicia for decade, was Francisco Franco's propaganda chief in 1960's and is only veteran of old fascist dictatorship who still holds high elected office in democratic Spain (M)

1999-12-22.title: Romania Sees Promise of Prosperity Slip Away
1999-12-22.abstract: Romania's economic prosperity, which almost vanished under dictatorship of Nicolae Ceausescu, grows even more remote 10 years after revolution that toppled Communism; often-overlooked agrarian country of 22 million, bigger than most Eastern European neighbors, is going through harder times than most; economy, in which any capitalist initiative was throttled by Stalinist system more severe than any East European regime outside Albania, has been unable to adapt to free-market system; middle class, vital for carrying out capitalist reforms, numbers less than 5 percent of population, compared with 30 percent in Czechoslovakia, 20 percent in Hungary and 15 percent in Poland; country's average wage has slipped, to $80 per month, and one-third of population lives on less than $2 per day; violent strikes reminiscent of Ceausescu's waning days have returned; government of Pres Emil Constantinescu--fragile and bickering multiparty coalition elected in 1997 with great hopes for change--is in turmoil; has been accused of corruption and of bungling economy; has not planned 10th anniversary celebration of revolution out of fear that any spark could set off rioting; photos; map (M)

1999-12-22.title: World Briefing
1999-12-22.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    ISRAEL: EXIT RULED OUT  The Latin Patriarch of Jerusalem said no emergency exit would be opened in the Holy Sepulcher Church, despite Israel's request for such an exit. The sects who share the site ''did not come to an agreement and the Israeli government said O.K.,'' the patriarch, Michel Sabbah, said. He also said the decision to build a small mosque next to the Basilica of the Annunciation in Nazareth is being reconsidered, despite the recent laying of a cornerstone.    Deborah Sontag (NYT)    EGYPT: PYRAMID PLANS REVERSED  Egypt has abandoned a plan to place a golden capstone atop the largest Pyramid at the height of millennium celebrations. Opposition newspapers have described the capstone as a Zionist symbol; archaeologists fear the cap could damage the structure.    Douglas Jehl (NYT)    JORDAN: ARAFAT'S HEALTH  Yasir Arafat is in ''very good'' health, his private physician said. The Palestinian leader ''was given a clean bill of health in a routine medical examination,'' said Dr. Ashraf al-Kurdi, a leading Jordanian neurologist. Doctors say a tremor in Mr. Arafat's lower lip is a nervous tic; there has been speculation that he has Parkinson's disease.   (AP)    THE AMERICAS    PERU: FUJIMORI MAY RUN  The election board accepted the application of President Alberto K. Fujimori's coalition, Peru 2000, for a place on the ballot in April, allowing him to run for a third term. Mr. Fujimori is expected to announce his candidacy next week. Mr. Fujimori has dismissed several judges who stood in the way of a re-election bid.   Clifford Krauss (NYT)    EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: POLITICAL MANEUVER  In a move aimed at benefiting Gerry Adams, president of Sinn Fein, the political wing of the Irish Republican Army, the government will introduce a law permitting politicians in the British Parliament and the Northern Ireland Assembly to run for seats in the Irish Parliament in Dublin. Mr. Adams is a member of the Belfast assembly and of the British Parliament, though he has not taken his seat because that would require him to pledge allegiance to the queen.   Warren Hoge (NYT)    BRITAIN: FAYED WINS LIBEL SUIT  A London jury found that Mohamed al-Fayed, the owner of Harrods, did not libel Neil Hamilton, left, a former Conservative member of the House of Commons, when he said he had made cash payments to Mr. Hamilton in exchange for parliamentary favors. Mr. Hamilton, who lost his seat in 1997 over the charges, must pay $1.6 million in court costs.    Warren Hoge (NYT)    PORTUGAL: RULING FAVORS HOMOSEXUALS  Homosexuality is no reason to ban a father from caring for his children, the European Court of Human Rights ruled. The judges found that Portugal had violated the rights to ''respect for private and family life'' of a divorced father when it stripped him of responsibility for his 9-year-old daughter because he is homosexual.    (Reuters)    MOLDOVA: NEW PREMIER  Parliament elevated the deputy economic minister to prime minister, breaking a political deadlock dating to Nov. 9, when legislators ousted the reform-oriented Ion Sturza in a no-confidence vote. The new leader, Dumitru Braghis, 42, faces an economic crisis that has led international lenders to suspend aid.    Michael Wines (NYT)    SPAIN: POLICE FOIL BOMB ATTACK  Officials said a routine traffic stop foiled a huge bomb attack by the armed Basque separatist group E.T.A. in Madrid. The police discovered a ton of explosives and a timing device near Calatayud in a white van that was on its way to the capital. The seizure came three weeks after E.T.A. ended its 14-month cease-fire.    Ben Jones (NYT)    CROATIA: JAN. 24 VOTE  Croats will vote for a new president on Jan. 24 to replace Franjo Tudjman, who died on Dec. 10, two years before his term ended. Mr. Tudjman failed to groom a successor despite widespread speculation for three years that he was dying. Opinion polls favor the moderate foreign minister Mate Granic, but his election is far from certain.    (AP)    ASIA    INDIA: REBELS GET A PASS  About 173 separatist guerrillas in Assam have accepted a state government offer allowing them safe passage for 10 days to meet their families on New Year's Eve, officials said. Leaders of militiant groups like the United Front of Assam and the National Democratic Front of Bodoland rejected the offer, but some guerrillas chose to accept it, officials said.   P. J. Anthony (NYT)    INDIA: SECURITY ALERT  Security in tribal areas in Gujarat, the scene of violence against Christians this year, has been stepped up for Christmas, a government statement said. Protection has been provided for 748 places in four districts, including churches and prayer halls, the statement said.    P. J. Anthony (NYT)    CHINA: PRESSED BY EUROPE ON RIGHTS  The European Union prodded China to ratify two international human rights pacts and raised concern over Beijing's crackdown on pro-democracy activists and the Falun Gong, and its treatment of Tibet. At the second annual summit meeting, top European officials also pressed Beijing to respond to demands to open its markets before beginning detailed talks on its entry to the World Trade Organization.    (Reuters)    AFRICA    NAMIBIA: UNITA FIRES BACK  Angolan rebels fired mortars into the village of Mbambi, killing a policeman and wounding three others, local media reported. Unita rebels were apparently responding to Namibia's decision to allow Angolan soldiers to use Namibia as a staging area for attacks on the rebels.      Rachel L. Swarns (NYT)    MOROCCO: ISRAELI TO VISIT  The Moroccan-born foreign minister of Israel, David Levy, will visit at the end of Ramadan in January at the invitation of King Mohammed, the Israeli foreign ministry said.    (Reuters)

1999-12-22.title: A Resignation Is Cheered By Feminists Around Japan
1999-12-22.abstract: Knock Yokoyama, once popular governor of Osaka, Japan's second largest city, resigns in major victory for women's rights as prosecutors file charges against him for molesting 21-year-old campaign worker; case has provided rare, high-profile instance of encouragement to Japanese women, who have traditionally been reluctant to bring sexually related complaints to court (M)

1999-12-23.title: Fear Erases Hope as Venezuelans Search for Dead
1999-12-23.abstract: Venezuela struggles to recover from its worst natural disaster of modern times, brought on by three days of torrential rain that set off vast landslides and flooding; suffering and death toll continue to mount; devastation along northern Caribbean coast is so widespread that authorities are still having difficulty quantifying it, one week after rains began; 150,000 people were driven from their homes; because of confusion about whereabouts of many, estimates of number of deaths range from 500 to 25,000; continued bad weather is hampering relief workers who have begun census; photo; map (M)

1999-12-23.title: London Journal; The Mayoral Frolics: What a Cast of Characters!
1999-12-23.abstract: Campaign for first directly elected mayor of London, Eng, has caught British politicians ill-prepared, with embarrassing consequences all around; there has been resignation of one candidate because of sex scandal and barring of another for his confessed serial adultery; because personality is playing more important role than party voting habits, contest has attracted hopefuls from nontraditional sectors; among them are two baronesses, gay rights activist, punk rock entrepreneur, Internet millionaire, novelist Jeffrey Archer and actress Glenda Jackson; photos (M)

1999-12-23.title: Rights Group Tells of Russian Abuses in Chechen Town
1999-12-23.abstract: Human Rights Watch, international human rights group, says refugees it interviewed from fighting in Chechnya said that Russian troops had pillaged small village of Alkhan-Yurt this month and killed 17 civilians; village is 10 miles from Grozny, which has been scene of fierce fighting; allegation by refugees emerges as important test of Russian military's credibility and its ability to maintain discipline in its ranks and to demonstrate concern for Chechnya's civilians; Russian military spokesmen deny that episode could have taken place, but Russian authorities appear to be conducting investigation into matter; photos (M)

1999-12-23.title: Italy's Semi-New Government, 57th Since the War, Is Sworn In
1999-12-23.abstract: Acting Prime Min Massimo D'Alema of Italy presents new, lightly shuffled cabinet that mostly underscores fierce infighting within his center-left majority that forced him to resign; new government is Italy's 57th since World War II; key members of D'Alema's team, including Foreign Min Lamberto Dini, Treasury Min Giuliano Amato and Economics Min Vincenzo Visco, remain in place; Sergio Mattarella is new defense minister (M)

1999-12-23.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-12-23.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: TALKS WITH U.S. -- Deputy Secretary of State Strobe Talbott discussed arms control and other issues with Prime Minister Vladimir V. Putin, but the two appeared to make little headway. The Defense Ministry again ridiculed United States proposals to alter the 1972 antiballistic missile treaty to permit a limited defense against rogue missiles, saying the treaty is the basis of arms control.   Michael Wines (NYT)    RUSSIA, BELARUS: PACT APPROVED -- Legislators in Russia and Belarus voted to ratify a treaty moving the two nations closer to economic union, but still well short of political merger. The treaty, signed this month by the Russian and Belarussian presidents, calls for a unified tax system by 2001 and a common currency by 2005, but is mostly short on details. It replaces a similar document signed in 1996.   Michael Wines (NYT)

1999-12-24.title: Jenne-Jeno Journal; Mali's Present Is Poor. Can It Build Future on Past?
1999-12-24.abstract: Dusan Vukovic has become popular figure in Serbia after denouncing war in Kosovo, in which his son, Aleksandar, died; angrily rejecting his son's posthumous medal at televised ceremony, Vukovic wanted it sent back to Pres Slobodan Milosevic; photo (M)

1999-12-24.title: U.S. Reports a Link To Algeria Terrorism
1999-12-24.abstract: Federal authorities said they had linked a couple who were arrested at a Vermont border crossing with a fake passport to a man associated with Algerian terrorists. But they did not specify what, if anything, they think the couple's plans were. In particular, they did not cite any connection with Ahmed Ressam, who was stopped as he tried to enter Washington State in a car with what officials said were bomb materials.    Since his indictment, Canada's relaxed immigration rules have come under scrutiny. He had managed to stay in Canada after asylum was refused and his deportation ordered.

1999-12-24.title: U.S. Linking Jailed Couple To Terrorists From Algeria
John Paton Davies, Diplomat Who Ran Afoul of McCarthy Over China, Dies at 91
1999-12-24.abstract: John Paton Davies, a leading diplomat who was among 'old China hands' driven from State Dept after Sen Joseph McCarthy questioned their loyalty and labeled them Communist sympathizers, dies at 91; Davies and about dozen of country's most eminent China scholars and diplomats found themselves accused of having 'lost China' after Mao's Communist forces defeated Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists, supported by American government, in 1949; photos (L)

1999-12-24.title: On New Year, Even Japan's Premier Will Be on Duty
1999-12-24.abstract: Prime Min Keizo Obuchi will reportedly remain cloistered with senior aides over New Year just in case major computer problems occur in Japan; Obuchi has been appearing on television for last two weeks, warning of computer mishaps even as he seeks to reassure public; thousands of soldiers, police officers and bureaucrats have also been called upon for New Year's weekend duty (M)

1999-12-25.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-12-25.abstract: EUROPE    BOSNIA: SERB SEIZED IN RAPES  A Bosnian Serb accused of taking part in the organized rape and torture of female Muslim prisoners was arrested by NATO troops and flown to the Yugoslav war crimes tribunal in The Hague. A statement by NATO's secretary general, Lord Robertson, identified the suspect as Zoran Vukovic and said he was seized on charges of committing crimes against humanity during the 1992-95 Bosnian war.   (AP)    LUXEMBOURG: GRAND DUKE TO STEP DOWN  Grand Duke Jean of Luxembourg, 78, one of Europe's longest-serving monarchs, will abdicate next year in favor of his oldest son, Prince Henri, 44, Prime Minister Jean-Claude Juncker announced. The grand duke has ruled Luxembourg, a country of 365,000 bordered by Belgium, France and Germany, since November 1964. The grand duke's role is largely ceremonial, but he must sign all bills before they can become law and he has the power to dissolve Parliament.   (AP)

1999-12-25.title: Hijackers Send Indian Jetliner On an Odyssey
1999-12-25.abstract: Five Sikh men hijack Indian Airlines jet and kill at least one of 189 people aboard, during forced odyssey across India, Pakistan, Afghanistan and Persian Gulf, before touching down in Kandahar, Afghanistan; Taliban, militia that rules Afghanistan, denies having anything to do with hijacking; says it is concerned for safety of passengers and that its soldiers have surrounded plane; Taliban has reportedly refused Indian request for rescue attempt, and also refused plea for asylum from hijackers; plane is being refueled, indicating it might continue its tortured journey; India urges Taliban to keep plane on ground; hijackers are said to be armed with grenades, rifles and knives; photos; map (M)

1999-12-25.title: Joao Figueiredo, 81, Dead; Revived Brazil's Democracy
1999-12-25.abstract: Gen Joao Baptista de Figueiredo, who restored democratic government to Brazil in 1985, dies at age 81; photo (M)

1999-12-25.title: Little Town of Bethlehem Not Lying Still on Millennium's Last Christmas
1999-12-25.abstract: Christian spirituality, Palestinian nationalism and party fever intermingle in newly refurbished town of Bethlehem on Christmas Eve; Yasir Arafat, Palestinian leader, attends midnight Mass, accompanied by assorted leftist world leaders; Christmas Day is marked with fireworks and Middle Eastern banquet; photos (M)

1999-12-25.title: In Outpouring of Venom, Mexico Lawmakers Battle Over Budget
1999-12-25.abstract: Mexico's governing party fails to win passage of its $128 billion budget proposal for next year; one opposition congressman who did vote for Pres Ernesto Zedillo's budget bill is showered with trash on floor of Congress by his infuriated colleagues, who accuse him of selling himself to government; budget must be approved by Dec 31 to avert constitutional crisis; rancor has grown so furious that opposition parties refused to authorize Zedillo to travel to Washington to meet with Pres Clinton; deadlock appears political, as country heads into presidential election (M)

1999-12-26.title: Koreans See Dark Scenario in a U.S. Precaution
1999-12-26.abstract: US decision to distribute 14,000 protective gas masks to dependents of American soldiers and embassy staff members living in Seoul raises concerns among South Koreans that despite its new diplomatic tone, North Korea is planning a major assault that will include chemical warfare; US insists that decision is not tied to any increased threat or impending attack by North Korea, but is rather part of Pentagon's effort to improve protection of noncombatants stationed overseas (M)

1999-12-26.title: Safe in U.S., Ex-Envoy Is Haunted By Romania
1999-12-26.abstract: Romanian government reaffirms death sentence of Mircea Raceanu, who is a former diplomat condemned to death as an American spy in 1989 and who was released under amnesty as last political prisoner of expiring Communist regime; Raceanu is perplexed that current government would embrace without reservations the conclusions of Ceausescu regime that he is guilty; group of Romanian intellectuals has asked that sentence be overturned because Raceanu was a committed anti-Communist fighter (M)

1999-12-26.title: Ivory Coast Coup Draws French Reply
1999-12-26.abstract: France says it wants to send small numbers of troops to Ivory Coast to protect Henri Konan Bedie, ousted president, and to evacuate roughly 20,000 French nationals should that become necessary, but country's new ruler, Gen Robert Guei, refuses entry to French reinforcements; Bedie has reportedly taken sanctuary with French garrison (M)

1999-12-26.title: Pope Urges Repentance, Tolerance And Respect
1999-12-26.abstract: John Paul II gives Christmas address on first day of Holy Year 2000, calling for repentance in the new millennium; pushes open Holy Door to St Peter's, symbol of beginning of a Holy Year, or Jubilee, and thousands of believers pass through it (M)

1999-12-26.title: John Taylor, 77, Top Editor for 'Jane's All the World's Aircraft'
1999-12-26.abstract: John W R Taylor, longtime editor of Jane's All The World's Aircraft, authoritative guide to aviation, dies at 77; photo (M)

1999-12-27.title: Ousted Leader Of Ivory Coast Flees to Togo
1999-12-27.abstract: Henri Konan Bedie, ousted president of Ivory Coast, flees to Togo aboard French aircraft; may seek asylum in France; three days of rioting nears end as army establishes control; US, Nigeria and South Africa denounce coup; Economic Community of West African States will meet to discuss crisis; coup leader, former armed forces chief Gen Robert Guei, says he will create government that will include civilians, but does not say when; photo; maps (M)

1999-12-27.title: Australians Face Influx of Immigrants Smuggled Ashore By Boat
1999-12-27.abstract: Australia is facing influx of illegal immigrants from Middle East, who officials say are smuggled to nearby Indonesian islands then moved to north coast of Australia where they try to claim asylum; immigration authorities report 34 boats with passengers originating in Middle East were intercepted off northern and northwestern coasts between July and November; boats carried 1,643 people, or more than total reaching country by boat in last three years; authorities say many refugees and asylum seekers seem to have been brought to Southeast Asia by smuggling rings operating in Amman, Jordan (M)

1999-12-27.title: Storm Kills 62 in France, Switzerland and Germany
1999-12-27.abstract: Fierce storm with hurricane-force winds barrels across northern France and parts of Switzerland and Germany, injuring and killing as many as 62 people; gale force winds in heart of Paris uproot trees, smash windows and cause other major damage; photos (M)

1999-12-27.title: CHINA SENTENCES 4 IN SPIRITUAL GROUP TO LONG JAIL TIME
1999-12-27.abstract: Beijing court sentences four members of Falun Gong spiritual movement to prison sentences ranging up to 18 years after one-day trial; severe sentences and prominent announcement on television underscores government's determination to crush sect; two sentences of 18 and 16 years are harsher than any given to leaders of banned China Democracy Party or any other democracy advocate in last several years; all four defendants are members of Communist Party; include Li Chang, official in Public Security Ministry who received 18 years, and Wang Zhiwen, engineer in Railways Ministry who received 16 years; others identified as Yao Jie, leader of party committee of large Beijing real estate company who received 12 years and Ji Liewu, manager of government metals company in Hong Kong who received 7 years (M)

1999-12-27.title: Citing Fear of Attacks, Burundi Herds 350,000 Hutu Into Camps
1999-12-27.abstract: Tutsi-dominated Government of Burundi forcibly relocates 350,000 Hutu into camps near capital of Bujumbura in latest turn in ethnic struggle between both tribes; cites need to end series of guerrilla attacks on capital since summer; hopes to deprive Hutu rebels of main source of food and shelter; squalid camps, which number nearly 60, have become human disaster; diseases such as cholera and dysentery abound and there is lack of security; international opposition to crisis has been relatively muted as foreign governments fear too much protest might hurt negotiations between government and rebels; crisis has created major quandry for international aid agencies, which are engaged in heated debate over whether they are actually encouraging camps by providing food, blankets and medical care; photos; map (M)

1999-12-28.title: RELIGIOUS FACTION SAYS IT WILL QUIT BARAK'S CABINET
1999-12-28.abstract: Israel's third-largest political party, Shas, says it will quit Prime Min Ehud Barak's government because it does not adequately support programs for poor; party's departure would throw governing coalition into chaos just as it moves into criticial period, facing key budgetary and peacemaking deadlines; Barak asks Shas to wait 24 hours to see what could be resolved, and party agrees; Shas, ultra-Orthodox group, has made so many threats to bolt Barak's coalition that many Israelis, including Barak, are skeptical that announcement is anything more than posturing; Shas's withdrawal would leave Barak with minority government of 51 out of 120 members of Legislature; small parties and Arab parties can be expected to keep Barak's government from falling, but Shas's withdrawal would be destabilizing event at sensitive moment; photo (M)

1999-12-28.title: Battered by Fierce Weekend Storm, Western Europe Begins an Enormous Cleanup Job
1999-12-28.abstract: Residents of swath of Europe hit by fierce storm try to pick themselves up; damage is formidibale from freak gales, which reached up to 110 miles in hour; France is hit hardest, with hundreds injured and at least 44 dead, most from collapsing roofs and falling trees; list of damaged historic sites includes Notre Dame Cathedral and palace at Versailles; there are also at least 40 weather-related deaths in Germany, Switzerland, Britain, Belgium, Spain and Italy (M)

1999-12-28.title: Health Sects in China Thrive, If Authorities See No Threat
1999-12-28.abstract: Hundreds of movements advocating qigong, invisible forces thought to fight disease, continue to operate in China, even as authorities pursue their harsh crackdown on one prominent offshoot, Falun Gong, and step up scrutiny of others; official acceptance of some qigong sects while others are crushed is part of two-decade, often tortuous effort by government to distinguish supposedly scientific, beneficial qigong from practices that are labeled superstitious and then curbed; Falun Gong, officially branded as superstitious 'evil cult,' was banned in July and four of its leaders have been given stiff prison sentences for allegedly telling ill followers that they could dispense with conventional medicine; Chinese officials are aware that mystical cults in past have erupted into huge, disruptive political forces, and that is what they fear; photo (M)

1999-12-28.title: Ivory Coast's Leader Offers Signs of Democratic Revival
1999-12-28.abstract: Flights to Ivory Coast resume after first coup in country's 39 years of independence, and dusk-to-dawn curfew is lifted; businesses operate normally, but banks are forbidden to transfer money abroad; Gen Robert Guei, coup's leader, gathers country's political parties and invites them to nominate members for his cabinet, raising hopes that democracy might be restored; but Guei issues subtle warning to political parties to 'avoid troubles'; still in question is how spontaneous military's mutiny was; Gen Guei's junta is full of supporters of Alassane Ouattara, former prime minister who had hoped to run against ousted Pres Henri Konan Bedie, but was being excluded on ground that he was not really citizen of Ivory Coast; there have been no demonstrations demanding Bedie's restoration; map (M)

1999-12-28.title: Russians Attack Chechen Rebels in Mountain Strongholds
1999-12-28.abstract: Russian forces make halting progress in another day of furious street fighting in Chechnya's capital city, Grozny; Russian troops are probing city's outer neighborhoods, dodging land mines and trading artillery fire with guerrillas holed up in trenches and basements; tens of thousands of civilians remain trapped in city, without heat or electricity and in fear for their lives; Chechen president, Aslan Maskhadov, vows that his rebel fighters will defend Grozny to the last man; photos; map (M)

1999-12-29.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-12-29.abstract: EUROPE    GERMANY: KOHL INVESTIGATION -- Prosecutors in Bonn plan a criminal investigation against former Chancellor Helmut Kohl, who has admitted that he kept secret bank accounts and received more than $1 million in undisclosed political contributions while in office. Dismissing demands from even his own party, the Christian Democratic Union, Mr. Kohl has refused to identify the donors. The prosecutor is expected to recommend an investigation on Wednesday; if there is no objection within two days from the head of Parliament, who has already urged an inquiry, work will begin immediately.   Edmund L. Andrews (NYT)    GERMANY: SENDING FOREIGN PRISONERS HOME -- Justice Minister Herta Daubler-Gmelin said foreigners convicted and jailed in Germany should be sent to their home countries to serve their sentences, provided lawful penal systems exist there. Foreigners and immigrants of German ancestry are among the ''marginal groups that often end up in prison'' and thus contribute to overcrowding, she said.   Victor Homola (NYT)

1999-12-29.title: 3rd Attempt to Free Pakistani Militant
1999-12-29.abstract: Hijacking of Indian Airlines jet is third attempt by militant associates of Masood Azhar, Pakistani cleric jailed in India since 1994 in fight over Indian control of Kashmir, to barter his release; photo (M)

1999-12-29.title: Seattle Cancels a Party On Fears of Terrorism
1999-12-29.abstract: WASHINGTON—  Mayor Rudolph Giuliani of New York said Tuesday that the decision by Seattle to cancel New Year's Eve festivities — the first such cancellation in the United States — represented a victory for terrorists, and he vowed that a huge celebration in New York would go on as planned.

1999-12-29.title: Russians See No Quick End in Chechnya
1999-12-29.abstract: Russian military, facing heavy rebel resistance, concedes Chechnya fighting could drag on for at least several more months; has begun to prepare public for war that may be longer and messier than expected; map; photo (M)

1999-12-29.title: Israel Hands Over the Remains Of 2 Guerrillas from Hezbollah
1999-12-29.abstract: Israel, in another gesture to Hezbollah after renewal of Israeli-Syrian talks, hands over bodies of two of group's guerrillas killed Dec 9 in fighting in southern Lebanon; military conflict continues, with Israeli jets and guerrillas striking targets in area; map (M)

1999-12-30.title: The Boy, 6, From Cuba: So Missed
1999-12-30.abstract: Neighbors in Cardenas, Cuba, cling to memory of Elian Gonzalez, 6-year-old boy at center of international tug of war between Cuba and United States immigration authorities; neighbors say they cannot understand why Elian's relatives in Miami are so intent on keeping boy against wishes of his father, Juan Miguel Gonzalez, who wants his son back; photos (M)

1999-12-30.title: Hijackers of Jet End 2 Demands Made to India
