1999-12-30.abstract: Hijackers holding 154 hostages in Indian Airlines jet at airport in Afghanistan drop two of their demands, but continue to insist that India release jailed Pakistani Muslim cleric and 35 others whom Indian government considers terrorists opposed to Indian rule in Kashmir; hijackers drop demand that India hand over $200 million and body of slain militant after representatives of Afghanistan's ruling Taliban militia tell hijackers they have violated conditions of landing in Afghanistan by adding demands for money and body; Taliban is playing increasingly important role in efforts to end six-day-old crisis; Indian government, which does not have diplomatic relations with Taliban, nevertheless praised Taliban for its efforts to negotiate with hijackers; map (M)

1999-12-30.title: AMERICAN TOPICS : Complaint About a Boss?There's a Web Site for You
1999-12-30.abstract: Web sites dedicated to the cult of complaint — allowing public- and private-sector employees to vent complaints against cruel, incompetent or even law-breaking bosses — appear to be proliferating on the Internet.

1999-12-30.title: Russian Outraged at Tapes on Pillage in Chechnya
1999-12-30.abstract: Russia's chief representative in Chechnya, Nikolai Koshman, is visibly troubled by what he sees in Alkhan-Yurt, village near Grozny that residents say was pillaged by Russian troops; tape recording of Koshman's visit to village strongly supports allegations that Russian troops went on rampage, terrorizing civilians, killing some and stealing their goods; at one point Koshman himself is threatened by Russian soldier, who backs down after being told Koshman is top Kremlin aide; Koshman promises to supply food and medicine to people of Alkhan-Yurt, and also promises thorough investigation; Maj Gen Vladimir A Shamanov, whose troops are in Alkhan-Yurt, denies that his soldiers committed any crimes; photo; map (M)

1999-12-30.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-12-30.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: LIBERAL TELLS OF ELECTION BAN  The head of the liberal Islamic opposition said all its candidates in the provinces had been barred from running in February's key parliamentary elections. ''It's the first stage in a long battle,'' Ibrahim Yazdi, secretary general of the Iran Freedom Movement, said after newspapers reported that 401 candidates of the 6,860 who applied to run had been rejected by the interior ministry. Mr. Yazdi said he had heard no word about the barring of Freedom Movement candidates in Tehran, where 30 seats are being contested.   (Agence France-Presse)    IRAQ: NEW FERRY LINK  A second ferry line linking Iraq to other nations in the Persian Gulf has opened with the arrival of a car ferry at the Iraqi port of Um Qasr from Bahrain. Iraq, barred from international air traffic under United Nations sanctions, was permitted to open the first ferry link in the gulf last year, from Dubai. The ferries take passengers, cars and some cargo, since each passenger is allowed about 220 pounds of baggage.   Barbara Crossette (NYT)

1999-12-31.title: Putin Asserts National Pride Is at Stake in Chechnya War
1999-12-31.abstract: Prime Min Vladimir V Putin says Russian national pride is at stake in war to retake rebellious Chechnya; Russian general say war is intensifying in regional capital of Grozny and along mountain passes to the south, where rebels are dug in; Chechen commanders concede losing ground in Grozny and the mountains, but Pres Aslan Maskhadov vows that Russians will not be able to conquer Chechnya even if the war goes on for 10 years (M)

1999-12-31.title: On Millennium's Heels, One for Jews
1999-12-31.abstract: Rabbi Schlomo Gestetner, director of Mayanot Institute of Jewish Studies in Jerusalem, a modern Hasidic organization, seeks to exploit millennial excitement without commemorating Jesus by organizing a Y6K benefit concert on New Year's Day, 240 years short of the arrival of sixth millennium of Jewish calendar; Jerusalem will be shut down on New Year's Eve, the Sabbath, and the millennium will be a nonevent for most people there; photo (M)

1999-12-31.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-12-31.abstract: THE AMERICAS    ARGENTINA: DRUG CHIEF'S WIDOW INDICTED  A judge has indicted the widow of Pablo Escobar, once leader of Colombia's Medellin drug cartel, on charges of money laundering and froze $10 million of her assets. Prosecutors said Victoria Henao Vallejos has led a money laundering organization in Argentina since she arrived in Buenos Aires almost two years ago using an alias granted by Colombia through a witness protection program.   Clifford Krauss (NYT)    CHILE: EX-PINOCHET AIDES WARNED  The foreign ministry warned 36 former aides to Gen. Augusto Pinochet not to leave Chile, telling them that the Spanish judge who issued the warrant for the former dictator's arrest in London is now planning to prosecute them as well. Foreign Minister Juan Gabriel Valdes told the officials, mostly high-ranking retired military officers, that the government could not protect them from prosecution if they left the country.   Clifford Krauss (NYT)

1999-12-31.title: Mexican Indians Lose Another Church Ally as Bishop Is Moved
1999-12-31.abstract: Vatican transfers Bishop Raul Vera Lopez to diocese in Mexican state of Coahuila, taking him from state of Chiapas, where he shared diocese with Bishop Samuel Ruiz Garcia, who has been an ardent advocate of rights of Indians and who hoped Vera would succeed him when he is forced to step down at age 75 (M)

1999-12-31.title: George Harrison Stabbed in Chest by an Intruder
1999-12-31.abstract: Man armed with large knife breaks into house of George Harrison, the former Beatle, in Hensley-on-Thames and stabs him in the chest; Harrison is treated for collapsed lung and various minor stab wounds; Harrison's wife, Olivia, is treated for cuts and bruises she suffered in struggle with intruder; police arrest a man, identified in news media at Michael Abram, and charge him on suspicion of attempted murder; evidence emerges that he may have harbored a dangerous obsession with Beatles; photos (M)
